---
title: "[FILL: Title of the paper]"
subtitle: "[FILL: Subtitle of the paper]"

author:
  - name: "[FILL: Author One]"
    affiliations:
      - name: "[FILL: University]"
        city: "[FILL: City]"
        country: "[FILL: Country]"
    orcid: "[FILL: 0000-0000-0000-0000]"
    email: "[FILL: email@example.com]"
    corresponding: true
  - name: "[FILL: Author Two]"
    affiliations:
      - name: "[FILL: University]"
        city: "[FILL: City]"
        country: "[FILL: Country]"
    orcid: "[FILL: 0000-0000-0000-0000]"

abstract: |
  [FILL: Write your abstract here. Summarize the research question, methods, key findings, and implications.]

keywords:
  - "[FILL: keyword 1]"
  - "[FILL: keyword 2]"
  - "[FILL: keyword 3]"

date: today

bibliography: references.bib
---

## Introduction {#sec-introduction}

Regional economic disparities remain a central concern in development economics.
Understanding the relationship between GDP per capita and life expectancy
across regions is critical for designing **effective policy interventions**.

This paper uses a novel synthetic dataset to examine these patterns,
employing both parametric and non-parametric methods [@keola2015monitoring].

Our results suggest a strong positive correlation between economic output
and health outcomes, with significant heterogeneity across geographic clusters.

This study contributes to the literature in two ways. First, we provide
a multi-language reproducible workflow. Second, we demonstrate how
*modern tools* can enhance transparency in empirical research.

The rest of this article is organized as follows.
@sec-literature provides an overview of the related literature.
@sec-data introduces the data and methods.
@sec-results presents the results and discussion.
Finally, @sec-conclusion offers some concluding remarks.

## Related literature {#sec-literature}

The relationship between income and health has been extensively studied
since the seminal work of @keola2015monitoring. Recent advances
in spatial econometrics have enabled more nuanced analyses of regional
disparities^[See also the World Bank regional development reports.].

## Data and methods {#sec-data}

[FILL: Describe the data sources, variables, and sample.]

[FILL: Explain the analytical methods and models used.]

### Data

[FILL: Describe the dataset. Include sources, time period, and key variables.]

The following figure, generated in [N1: Sample Data Exploration](notebooks/notebook-01.ipynb), provides an overview of the data.

{{< embed notebooks/notebook-01.ipynb#fig-sample >}}

The following table reports summary statistics by region.

{{< embed notebooks/notebook-01.ipynb#tbl-summary >}}

The same analysis is replicated in R ([N2: Sample R Analysis](notebooks/notebook-02.ipynb)) and Stata ([N3: Sample Stata Analysis](notebooks/notebook-03.ipynb)) to
demonstrate multi-language support.

{{< embed notebooks/notebook-02.ipynb#fig-r-sample >}}

{{< embed notebooks/notebook-02.ipynb#tbl-r-summary >}}

{{< embed notebooks/notebook-03.ipynb#fig-stata-sample >}}

{{< embed notebooks/notebook-03.ipynb#stata-summary >}}

### Methods

[FILL: Describe the econometric or statistical methods.]

## Results and discussion {#sec-results}

[FILL: Present and interpret the main findings.]

[FILL: Discuss implications and compare with existing literature.]

## Concluding remarks {#sec-conclusion}

[FILL: Summarize the main findings and their implications.]

[FILL: Discuss limitations and directions for future research.]

## Acknowledgments {.appendix}

[FILL: Acknowledge funding sources, collaborators, and data providers.]

## Data and code availability {.appendix}

The data and code used in this study are available at [FILL: repository URL].

## References {.unnumbered}
